writing_task: I need help drafting a short lapsed-donor reactivation email — just 3 paragraphs. These are mid-level donors ($500–$2,500 range) who gave to our workforce training program two or three years ago but haven't renewed. I want to remind them what their earlier investment actually produced, introduce a specific person whose trajectory connects back to that funding, and close with a concrete ask tied to where the program is now. Nothing guilt-trippy — I want them to feel like they made a smart call back then and there's a reason to re-engage, not like they abandoned us.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: What you funded in 2022 — and what Marcus did with it

When you put $1,000 toward our welding certification cohort in 2022, you were essentially betting on twelve people who'd signed up for a six-week program most of their employers didn't yet know existed. Marcus Reyes was one of them. He'd been working warehouse temp jobs for four years, decided the ceiling was too low, and showed up the first morning twenty minutes early with his own notebook. He finished the cohort, passed his AWS certification on the first attempt, and took a position at Callahan Fabrication eight days later at $24 an hour — nearly double what he'd been making.

That cohort ran because donors like you chose to fund the instructor hours and materials directly. The program didn't expand itself — our training director, Lena Okafor, spent two years recruiting industry partners willing to hire graduates before the program had a graduation record to point to. That groundwork is what made Marcus's timeline possible. Today, Lena is running three concurrent cohorts and has 27 people on a waiting list who applied for the spring session.

Those 27 people are waiting because we can credential roughly 14 more this year than we have funding to train. A gift of $750 to $1,500 covers one participant's full instruction and materials — the same investment you made before. If you want to get back in, Lena can tell you exactly which trade track your gift would fund. You can reach her directly at lokafor@[org].org, or give online at [link].
drafts.generic:
Two years ago, your gift helped us train over 140 workers in our region — and Marcus was one of them. He came in unemployed, completed our twelve-week machining certification, and just accepted a lead technician role at a local manufacturer. His story isn't unusual; it's what your investment was designed to produce.

We're expanding the program this fall to serve 60 additional participants, including a new cohort focused on clean energy trades. The infrastructure is ready. What we need now are the partners who helped build the foundation.

Would you consider renewing your support with a gift of $750 or more? Your previous investment already proved its worth — this is simply the next chapter.
drafts.preference:
A few years ago, your gift helped us launch something that honestly exceeded what we hoped for — our workforce training cohorts grew, completion rates climbed, and people who'd been stuck found real footing. One of those people was Marcus, who entered the program unemployed and left with a welding certification and a job offer the same week he graduated. He's since been promoted twice and recently came back to mentor a current participant. That arc started somewhere, and your investment was part of it.

Marcus isn't an outlier. He's representative of what this program consistently produces when it's properly funded.

We're now enrolling our largest cohort yet and looking for partners who already understand what this work can do. Would you consider renewing your support with a gift of $750 this year? Your contribution would directly fund training materials and employer-connection services for participants in this new group — people ready to do exactly what Marcus did.

user_reason: C is the clear winner — Marcus has a name, a specific decision he made, a number that makes his outcome tangible ($24/hour, eight days later), and the ask ties directly back to the story with a specific cost-per-outcome. Lena is the one running cohorts, not 'we launched' anything. The donor framing treats them like someone who made a smart bet, not a benefactor. B is solid — Marcus mentoring others is a nice arc, and 'partners who already understand what this work can do' is exactly the right tone. But 'your gift helped us launch something' puts the org as subject, and the impact claim about completion rates climbing is vague. The ask is good but less specific than C. A is the weakest — it opens with a number before the story, which is backwards. 'We're expanding' and 'we need' make the org the subject repeatedly. 'The infrastructure is ready' is corporate-adjacent. The ask is the most generic of the three. A reads like a competent fundraising email; C reads like something I'd actually want in my outbox.

criteria_analysis.name: Active Human Agency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here: Marcus 'decided,' 'showed up,' 'passed'; Lena 'spent two years recruiting'; the donor 'chose to fund' — every significant action has a human subject making a choice. The generic draft uses passive/subjectless constructions like 'We're expanding the program' and 'The infrastructure is ready' with no human driving expansion. The original preference draft is better ('Marcus entered,' 'He's since been promoted') but still uses subjectless constructions like 'completion rates climbed' and 'cohorts grew' without attributing growth to anyone.

criteria_analysis.name: Human-Centered Specificity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names Marcus in the first paragraph with vivid observable details (arrived twenty minutes early with his own notebook, warehouse temp jobs for four years, $24/hour), introduces Lena Okafor as a second named person, and zooms out through their experiences rather than abandoning them. The generic draft names Marcus but provides almost no concrete, observable detail — 'came in unemployed, completed our twelve-week machining certification' is thin and generic. The original preference draft gives Marcus a story but lacks the grounding observable details (no specific wages, no concrete first-day moment) and uses him partly as decoration for program-level claims.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens by naming the exact amount ('$1,000'), the exact thing funded ('welding certification cohort'), the exact year, and draws a causal line to Marcus's outcome — the donor is framed as someone who made a traceable, smart bet. The generic draft uses vague 'your gift helped us train over 140 workers' and 'your investment already proved its worth' — abstract support language this user explicitly rejects. The original preference draft is better ('your gift helped us launch something') but still leans on vague 'your investment was part of it' language rather than naming the specific mechanism.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete Over Evocative
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is packed with specific facts: $1,000, six-week program, AWS certification, eight days, $24/hour, three concurrent cohorts, 27-person waiting list, $750–$1,500 per participant, 14 more trainees than funding covers. The generic draft offers almost no specific numbers — '140 workers,' '60 additional participants,' and '$750 or more' are its only data points, and they feel unanchored. The original preference draft uses 'promoted twice' and '$750' but lacks the density of concrete detail, relying instead on evocative but vague phrases like 'people who'd been stuck found real footing.'

criteria_analysis.name: Tonal Consistency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft maintains a grounded, warm, direct register throughout — even the ask paragraph stays in the same voice, naming Lena personally and keeping the language plain. The generic draft breaks register sharply: 'The infrastructure is ready. What we need now are the partners who helped build the foundation' is classic newsletter-speak that this user would flag immediately. The original preference draft is tonally warm and consistent but has a slight wobble with 'Marcus isn't an outlier. He's representative of what this program consistently produces when it's properly funded' — which edges toward organizational messaging.

criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft builds from Marcus's story through Lena's expansion to the 27 waiting people and a specific funding gap (14 more than they can train), closing with a concrete contact and dollar range — the ask grows organically from the narrative and leaves a door open with specific unfinished work. The generic draft's ask ('Would you consider renewing your support') arrives with no narrative momentum or specific gap — 'this is simply the next chapter' is a cliché closer. The original preference draft connects its ask to Marcus's story better but the gap is less specific ('our largest cohort yet') and the ending, while warm, doesn't leave as tangible a thread to pull.

The original preference draft is solid and warm but lacks the specificity density and active human agency that the rubric-guided draft achieves. Draft A follows a tight logical arc: your past gift → what it funded → a specific person's journey → the staff who made it possible → current need → how to act. Draft B follows a similar arc but the connections feel more compressed, with less connective tissue between Marcus's story and the ask.
Both drafts are mechanically clean with no grammatical errors, typos, or punctuation issues.

Draft A has three well-delineated paragraphs each with a distinct function (story, context/credibility, ask). Draft B's three paragraphs serve similar functions but the middle paragraph is thin and the overall structure feels slightly underdeveloped.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is substantially more engaging and persuasive due to its precise, textured storytelling, clear attribution of impact to specific people and actions, and a concrete ask with a direct contact — all while maintaining excellent clarity and organization. Draft B is competent but reads like a polished template rather than a personalized, compelling appeal.

writing_task: I need help drafting a short thank-you note — just 2–3 paragraphs — for first-time donors who gave during our year-end campaign. These are people who gave at the $100–$250 level, so they're not major donors yet, but we want to convert them into recurring givers. The note should acknowledge what their specific gift made possible (we'll say it went toward our job training program) and leave them feeling like they made a genuinely smart decision — not just a kind one. Can you draft something that opens with a specific person from the program rather than a generic "thank you for your gift"?

drafts.rubric:
Marcus showed up to his first session with a notebook he'd bought the night before — still had the price tag on it. Seven months later, he passed his CompTIA certification and accepted a network technician position at $47,000 a year. That's not a small shift. That's a different life, and he drove every mile of it.

Your $[amount] covered the instructor hours that made his cohort possible. Specifically: twelve weeks of hands-on training, materials, and the certification exam fee for six participants. Marcus was one of them. That's the transaction, and it was a good one.

Right now, 23 people are on the waitlist for the next cohort. We have funding for eight seats. If you decide to give monthly — even at the same level — you'd cover the certification exam fees for two additional participants per cycle. Marcus would probably tell you that's worth it. We think you already know it is.
drafts.generic:
When Marcus walked into our job training program last spring, he hadn't worked in three years. Six months later, he accepted a full-time position in IT support — with benefits. Your gift during our year-end campaign helped make that possible, funding the instruction, tools, and one-on-one coaching that moved him from uncertainty to a paycheck.

That's not charity. That's leverage. Every dollar invested in job training returns measurably to the community through wages earned, taxes paid, and families stabilized. You made a smart bet on real outcomes.

We'd love to keep you in the loop as the program grows this year. If you're open to staying involved — even a small monthly gift keeps momentum going — we'll make sure you see exactly what your continued support builds.
drafts.preference:
Marcus came into our job training program last spring with a gap on his résumé and a lot of uncertainty about what came next. By fall, he'd earned his forklift certification and accepted an offer with a local logistics company. Your gift helped make that possible — not in a vague, general way, but in a real, specific way: funding the equipment time, the instruction hours, and the support that kept him enrolled.

That's the thing about giving at this level — it's targeted enough to actually move something. Your $[amount] didn't disappear into overhead. It went to work.

If you're open to it, we'd love to invite you to continue that impact with a monthly gift — even $20 a month keeps a participant enrolled through a full certification cycle. You can set that up easily at [link], and we'd be grateful to have you with us beyond this one season.

user_reason: Draft A is the clear winner. Marcus is the subject of his own story — 'he drove every mile of it' — and the framing never slips into the non-profit as hero. The ask is surgically connected to the story: certification exam fees, two participants per cycle, tied directly to what the donor's money actually covered. The '23 on the waitlist, funding for 8 seats' is exactly the kind of specific number paired with human context I want. It also frames the donor as someone making a smart transaction, not a generous soul. Draft B is decent but softer — 'we'd love to invite you' and 'we'd be grateful' creep into benefactor framing, and the ask ('even $20 a month keeps a participant enrolled') is slightly more generic than A's ask. The forklift/logistics pivot also feels less specific than A's CompTIA detail. Draft C has real problems: Marcus is described as someone things happened to ('helped make that possible'), the non-profit is implicitly the hero, and the ask is vague — 'keeps momentum going' tells me nothing. The 'that's not charity, that's leverage' line tries to do what A does naturally but feels like a pitch rather than earned framing. The closing is the weakest of the three — no specific outcome tied to continued giving.

criteria_analysis.name: Active Human Agency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here: Marcus 'showed up,' 'passed,' 'accepted' — every milestone has him as the active subject, and the donor is framed as making a specific covering decision. The generic draft falls into passive/institutional framing ('Your gift helped make that possible,' 'Every dollar invested returns') where the program and abstract forces do the work. The original preference draft is better than generic ('Marcus came into,' 'he'd earned,' 'accepted an offer') but still lets the gift 'help make that possible' vaguely and uses 'it went to work' with the dollar as subject rather than a person.

criteria_analysis.name: Human-Centered Specificity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with Marcus and a vivid, observable detail (notebook with price tag still on), keeps him as the structural backbone, and threads the ask back through him ('Marcus would probably tell you'). The generic draft names Marcus but gives almost no observable detail — 'hadn't worked in three years' is context, not a pictureable moment — and abandons him entirely by paragraph two. The original preference draft names Marcus with decent detail (gap on résumé, forklift certification, logistics company) but the ask disconnects from his story and becomes generic program language.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names the specific mechanism ('instructor hours that made his cohort possible'), itemizes what the gift covered ('twelve weeks of hands-on training, materials, certification exam fee for six participants'), calls the transaction a 'good one' (donor competence), and frames the recurring ask with exact math ('certification exam fees for two additional participants per cycle'). The generic draft uses 'funding the instruction, tools, and one-on-one coaching' which is somewhat specific but then drifts into 'smart bet on real outcomes' — flattery masquerading as specificity. The original preference draft names equipment time and instruction hours but then says 'it went to work,' which is vague, and the ask ('even $20 a month keeps a participant enrolled') is decent but doesn't make the donor feel like they already know how to do this.

criteria_analysis.name: Tonal Consistency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft maintains its grounded, direct register from the notebook detail through the ask — 'That's the transaction, and it was a good one' is the same voice as the opening, and 'Marcus would probably tell you' keeps the warmth human. The generic draft breaks register badly: it opens with Marcus's story, then pivots to 'That's not charity. That's leverage' (press-release language) and 'returns measurably to the community through wages earned, taxes paid' (newsletter-speak), then closes with 'We'd love to keep you in the loop' (institutional). The original preference draft is mostly consistent but 'That's the thing about giving at this level' and 'we'd be grateful to have you with us beyond this one season' slip into institutional warmth that feels different from the opening's specificity.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Forward Momentum
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft provides exact forward momentum: 23 people on a waitlist, funding for 8 seats, and a specific recurring gift covering two exam fees per cycle — the gap is concrete and the ask grows directly from Marcus's story. The generic draft ends with a vague 'we'll make sure you see exactly what your continued support builds' — no specific gap, no concrete thread. The original preference draft offers '$20 a month keeps a participant enrolled through a full certification cycle,' which is decent specificity, but it doesn't leave a tangible unfinished thread the way a waitlist number does.

The original preference draft is solid but loses energy in the ask and slips into mild institutional voice. The generic draft breaks register, abandons Marcus, and replaces specifics with abstractions — exactly the patterns this user identifies as failures.

Draft A's three paragraphs serve distinct, clear functions: story, impact accounting, ask. Draft B is also well-organized but its middle paragraph shifts from narrative to abstract argument ('leverage,' 'returns measurably'), which slightly dilutes the structural focus.

writing_task: I need help drafting a 2–3 paragraph mid-campaign update email for donors who gave at the $100–$250 level during our current back-to-school drive. We're halfway through the campaign and hitting our targets, but I want to give these donors a real progress update — not just a thermometer graphic and a "we're 50% there!" — so I'm hoping to anchor it around one specific family or student. The tone should feel like a genuine check-in, not a second ask, even though there's a soft re-engagement hook at the end for donors who might want to increase their impact before the campaign closes.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: A quick note from the middle of the campaign

When Marcus walked into our distribution site last Tuesday, he already had a list. He'd spent the night before writing down exactly what his two kids needed — backpacks, composition notebooks, a scientific calculator his seventh-grader's teacher had listed as required. He didn't want to forget anything, and he didn't want to ask twice. That list is sitting on our coordinator Diane's desk right now, fully checked off, because you funded the supply kits that made it possible. Specifically, your gift covered the per-family kit cost of $47, which included that calculator.

We're halfway through the campaign, and 214 families have walked through that door so far — about half the 430 we're aiming to reach before the drive closes on August 30th. Diane and her team have been running intake six days a week to keep pace. The families showing up now tend to be the ones who heard about it later, often through word of mouth from neighbors like Marcus, which means the second half of the campaign typically draws more first-time visitors who need more help navigating what's available.

There are still roughly 216 families on the schedule, and the supplies are there to meet them. What's less certain is whether we'll have enough coordinator hours to staff the extended sessions Diane is pushing to add in the final week — each additional session costs about $180 in staff time and reaches around 18 families. If you've been looking for a specific place to add to what you've already done, that's the gap. Either way, Marcus's kids started school this week with everything on the list.
drafts.generic:
When you gave last month, you made it possible for families like the Garcias to walk into this school year ready. Maria, a third-grader at Riverside Elementary, started classes on Tuesday with a backpack stocked with everything on her supply list — pencils, folders, a new pair of sneakers — things her mom told us she quietly worried about all summer. That moment happened because of you, and it happened on time.

We're midway through the drive, and the response has been stronger than we expected. More than 340 students have been served so far, and we're on track to reach every child on our list before the first bell rings.

If you've been thinking about doing a little more before we close on the 30th, there's still time to extend that impact. But whether or not you do, we wanted you to know exactly where things stand — and to say thank you, again, for being part of this from the start.
drafts.preference:
Subject: A quick update from the middle of the campaign

We're halfway through our back-to-school drive, and we wanted to check in with a real moment from the ground — not just a progress bar, but an actual glimpse of what's happening. Last week, our team met with a family of three in the Eastside neighborhood. Their youngest, a seven-year-old named Marcus, started second grade this week with a full backpack for the first time. New pencils, a sturdy binder, a pair of sneakers that fit. His mom told our coordinator he'd been asking about school supplies since June. Your gift helped make that happen.

We're on track to reach our goal by the end of the month, which is genuinely good news — it means more families like Marcus's will have what they need before the first bell rings. We're still working through our list of registered students, and every week that passes matters because some schools open before our campaign closes.

If you've been thinking about whether to do a little more before we wrap up, this is a good moment. A visit to our campaign page will show you exactly where things stand and let you add to your gift if it feels right. Either way, thank you for being part of this from the start — it means more than a thermometer graphic can show.

user_reason: C is the strongest by a significant margin — Marcus is the subject of his own story, he's acting with agency (he made a list, he didn't want to forget anything, he didn't want to ask twice), and the ask at the end ties directly to coordinator hours and a specific dollar amount per session, which is exactly the kind of specific-outcome-to-specific-number connection I want. The story comes first, then the number as scale evidence. The framing around Diane and the coordinator hours also avoids making the org the hero. The only issue is it cuts off mid-sentence, which is frustrating but fixable. B is solid — the framing of 'you made it possible for families like the Garcias' is close to the collaborator framing I prefer, and the story is warm without being manipulative. But 'things her mom told us she quietly worried about all summer' edges toward guilt-tripping, and the ask is too generic — 'extend that impact' doesn't connect back to Maria or the Garcias specifically. A is the weakest. 'Your gift helped make that happen' and 'thank you for being part of this' are benefactor-framing, not collaborator-framing. Marcus is mostly passive — things happened to him rather than him doing anything. And 'We're still working through our list' leads with the org as subject doing the work, which is exactly the framing I try to avoid.

criteria_analysis.name: Active Human Agency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels: Marcus walks in, writes a list, makes choices; Diane runs intake and pushes for extended sessions; the donor funded specific kits. The generic draft opens with 'you made it possible' which is fine, but then shifts to passive framing ('340 students have been served') and the org narrating its own success ('the response has been stronger than we expected'). The original preference draft has some agency (Marcus asking about supplies, the team meeting a family) but also uses passive constructions ('families like Marcus's will have what they need') and the org narrates its own work more than necessary.

criteria_analysis.name: Human-Centered Specificity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names Marcus in the first sentence with vivid observable detail (the list he wrote the night before, the specific items, the calculator), and his story is the structural backbone through which the campaign data and the ask emerge. The generic draft names Maria but with less distinctive detail (generic supply list items, 'quietly worried' is told not shown) and her story gets abandoned by paragraph two. The original preference draft names Marcus with good detail (asking about supplies since June) but introduces him as 'a seven-year-old named Marcus' which is more decorative than structural, and the broader context disconnects from him somewhat.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor as Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is outstanding: it names the specific mechanism ($47 per-family kit cost including the calculator), draws a traceable causal link, and positions the donor as the variable for the gap ($180 per session reaching ~18 families) without guilt or flattery. The generic draft uses 'you made it possible' and 'extend that impact' — abstract and vague, no specific mechanism named. The original preference draft says 'your gift helped make that happen' which is similarly vague, and the soft ask ('a visit to our campaign page') positions the org as gatekeeper rather than giving the donor a specific decision to make.

criteria_analysis.name: Tonal Consistency
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft maintains its grounded, factual, warm register from start to finish — no sentence breaks into newsletter-speak, and the organizational 'we' never appears. The generic draft starts warmly but shifts to 'the response has been stronger than we expected' (org narrating its own success) and 'we wanted you to know' (institutional voice). The original preference draft has register breaks: 'not just a progress bar, but an actual glimpse' is meta-narration about its own communication strategy, and 'it means more than a thermometer graphic can show' is the kind of winking self-awareness that breaks the intimate tone.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete Over Evocative
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is loaded with traceable, specific data: $47 per kit, 214 of 430 families served, 216 remaining, $180 per session, ~18 families per session, August 30th close date. Every number connects to information already established. The generic draft offers one number (340 students) with no traceability and replaces specifics with vague impact language ('extend that impact'). The original preference draft has no numbers at all — no costs, no counts, no waitlist data — relying instead on evocative detail ('new pencils, a sturdy binder, a pair of sneakers that fit') without the concrete facts this user consistently prioritizes.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Forward Momentum
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft leaves a specific, tangible door open: the coordinator-hours gap, priced at $180 per session reaching 18 families, with the donor positioned as the variable — then closes on Marcus's kids starting school, honoring progress without wrapping it up. The generic draft's ending ('there's still time to extend that impact') is a generic appeal with no specific thread to pull. The original preference draft points to the campaign page, which is slightly more concrete, but 'if it feels right' and the vague framing of what remains undone don't give the donor a specific gap to fill.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is clearly the strongest across every criterion — it embodies this user's core priorities: named people making choices, traceable data, the donor as a strategic decision-maker, and zero organizational self-narration. The original preference draft has better human-centered storytelling than the generic draft but falls short on specificity, donor mechanism, and tonal consistency. The generic draft reads like competent nonprofit boilerplate — exactly the kind of writing this user's rubric is designed to avoid.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A provides precise, concrete details (exact numbers, costs, dates) that make the situation unmistakably clear. Draft B is clear but more general, leaving the reader with a softer picture of the campaign's status.

Draft A moves seamlessly from an individual story to campaign-wide progress to a specific ask, with each paragraph logically building on the last. Draft B follows a similar arc but the transition from the anecdote to the midway update feels slightly thinner.
Both drafts are essentially flawless in grammar, punctuation, and mechanics.

Draft A has a tight three-paragraph structure — story, progress update, specific gap/ask — each with a clear purpose. Draft B is well-organized but its brevity means the middle section is comparatively underdeveloped.

Draft A's specificity (the handwritten list, the $47 kit, the calculator) makes the story vivid and the stakes tangible. Draft B's anecdote is warm but somewhat more generic ('pencils, folders, a new pair of sneakers') and less distinctive.

Both drafts strike an excellent tone — respectful, warm, and direct without being manipulative. Draft A is slightly more matter-of-fact; Draft B is slightly more effusive. Both are consistent and appropriate for a donor update.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece overall because it combines a compelling personal story with precise data and a clearly defined, specific ask, making it both more engaging and more actionable. Draft B is polished and pleasant but less distinctive and less informative.
